Hi,

Glad you were able to locate the correct documentation for what you needed. Sorry for the delay in responding. For zdesk's part in this, the query parameters do not appear in the documentation such that they would be currently picked up by the API generator. The only parameter picked up by the `search` endpoint is `query`. However, any additional parameters passed as `kwargs` will be passed on (either `sort_order` or `order_by`, whatever is correct currently).

I've considered attempting to parse the tables to provide further convenience, but you can see how even this effort can end up being a fruitless endeavor if the docs are not right.

But, please do report anything the API is generating that is blatantly incorrect or reasonably missing. I do patch the docs for typos in the endpoints themselves, for example.

Thanks,

Brent W.

* Thats just the parameters to infer the shape of the insert size distribution. There is no "reasonable range" It depends on how the library is constructed.  [this section](https://github.com/ksahlin/BESST#my-mate-pair-libraries-contains-paired-end-contamination-what-should-i-do) shows two different libraries with very different ranges.
* None means that parameter -e is not set, so BESST infers in dynamically based on the graph complexity in the region.
* These numbers doesn't look stranger than what I've seen before. Yes, the non unique reads looks a little high, but from your N50 I assume that you are dealing with a pretty complex assembly. The rest of the numbers looks completely fine. Coverage of 6 is ok given that not too much is contributed from PE contamination, because span coverage will be high. What is the fraction of PE contamination (should be printed somewhere close to the lines you pasted)?
* The final output is in passX if there are X libraries. So yes, 3 in your case.

So, I went ahead and tried this out in a simple notebook with the `%debug` magic. On iPython 3.x/4.x, it is smart enough to note the cell is empty and gives you a warning then continues on to the next cell. As a result, the kernel does not block. Only on iPython 2.x, this results in blocking when the cell is empty. The simple answer is use a newer version of iPython for this particular case.

As for the more general case of debug cells, these cells are designed to be blocking interactive elements. In other words, they want real time user interaction as opposed to generating some webpage components that can be interacted with later. As a result, I don't see a clear way to support running these in batch. As a result, I propose setting a variable that you can check in the notebook to see if you are running with `runipy`. For more details see this issue ( https://github.com/paulgb/runipy/issues/110 ).

I've been working on this for a long time to try to fix bugs, and the complexity is just crazy. Just figuring out the correct behavior is hard. To give a preview of the problem:

Let's say we register the shortcut `alt+w` with key suppression. It's a single modifier plus a single key, in a single step. one of the simplest possible examples. Now think what happens inside the OS hook, where we have to decide to block or allow each event that is captured.

The user presses `alt`. We have to either allow or block this event. We allow it, because we don't know if the next key will be our shortcut or not.

Then comes a "`w` down". Hey, that's our shortcut! We block this event, and also the "`w` up" that follows.

Now comes the "`alt` up". If we block it, the system will be left with a held down `alt`, wreaking havoc. If we allow it, the underlying program will receive a press-and-release of `alt` alone, sending the focus to the menu bar.

That's not good, so we decide to block the initial "`alt` down".

Then instead of `w` comes a "`tab` down", for `alt+tab`. We have to let that through, but we blocked the previous `alt`. So we send a fake "`alt` down", then allow the "`tab` down". But when the "`alt` up" event comes, we have to remember if we blocked the initial event (yes) and if we had to fake it later (yes). In a sequence of events like `alt+tab, alt+shift+m, alt+w, alt+tab` the logic gets crazy quick.

Then you realize that pressing `a` then `alt` should result in both `a` and `alt` being allowed and the shortcut not triggered (try `ctrl+a` versus `a+ctrl`).

And if someone is playing a game where `alt` is bound to any important action, they will definitely notice that holding down the key not doing anything until its released.

And **then** you realize there's actually three alts: `alt`, `left alt` and `right alt`...

Took *much* longer than expected, but the `suppress` branch has been merged and v0.13.1 includes fixes for this and many other bugs. You can now hold <kbd>shift</kbd> and mash <kbd>q</kbd> as much as you want.

Modifiers are also treated differently, so holding <kbd>q</kbd>and mashing <kbd>shift</kbd> doesn't and shouldn't work, but <kbd>a+b</kbd> is ok in either order.

The issue is that the `Transactional` class is completely agnostic about the attribute's nature. It just compares what is set during the transaction by the user to the feedback from the OS. If the values match each other, OK, otherwise transaction fails.

In some cases it is required to engage some custom match functions, like here: https://github.com/svinota/pyroute2/blob/0.4.18/pyroute2/ipdb/interfaces.py#L77

The approach I tried first in the patch above was too expensive, let alone it wasn't complete, as you mentioned. I'll revert the patch and will try to use this custom match functions. Thanks for the feedback!

@dpkp , here's what I used to test the connections before and after my changes:
```python
from kafka import KafkaProducer
params = ...
producer = KafkaProducer(**params)
msg = 'Hello, World!'
producer.send(cfg['topic'], bytes(msg))
producer.flush()
producer.close()

The only thing that needs to be changed between PLAIN and SASL authentication are the parameters passed to the producer. Without SASL, I used these parameters:
```python
params = {
  'bootstrap_servers': ['broker1:9092','broker2:9092'],
  'security_protocol': 'PLAINTEXT',
}
```
To connect to a broker using SASL, the parameters need to be changed to this:
```python
params = {
  'bootstrap_servers': ['broker1:9092','broker2:9092'],
  'security_protocol': 'SASL_PLAINTEXT',
  'sasl_mechanism': 'GSSAPI',
}
```

To successfully authentication with the broker the user need to acquire a valid Kerberos ticket. Both of the methods below work fine with the Python example:
1. Kinit manually beforehand
```
kinit alice
python test-producer.py
```
2. Set the KRB5_CLIENT_KTNAME env variable pointing to a valid keytab
```
export KRB5_CLIENT_KTNAME=/home/alice/alice.keytab
python test-producer.py
```

Adding a keytab option to kafka-python would be a plus but not essential, since this can be easily worked around as per above. Currently the QoP level set by this patch is hard-coded to 'auth', since that's the only one supported by Kafka. I could be easily parameterized later when/if Kafka is extended to support them. I'm not sure if this in the roadmap, though, since it already supports SASL_SSL, which provides integrity and confidentiality.

I'm not thrilled with this, but `(\s|-|^)` has been changed to `([\s(\["'-]|^)` to avoid a few possibly common prefix characters for which we might still consider a number as a time. Attempting to create an exhaustive whitelist of characters in a regex is not a great situation to be in, so this is certainly a case where we're striving for "good enough."

This might more appropriately fall under the umbrella of a feature that I plan to propose for a future version to allow avoiding uncertain matches (e.g. *04/05/2014* is obviously a date, but *1030* is not always – even not *usually* – a time).

We don't plan to remove old imports before version 1.0.0 (we may keep them even after 1.0.0 if we don't introduce more serious API changes) so I'd say it's OK to keep `astor.codegen` imports in the foreseeable future.

unixODBC

First I removed system packages unixODBC and unixODBC-dev (names may
vary by Linux distro). Then

```
wget ftp://ftp.unixodbc.org/pub/unixODBC/unixODBC-2.3.0.tar.gz
tar zxvf unixODBC-2.3.0.tar.gz
cd unixODBC-2.3.0
./configure
make
sudo make install
```

As root ensure that /usr/local/etc/odbcinst.ini has the following
entries:

```
[FreeTDS]
Driver          = /usr/local/lib/libtdsodbc.so

[SQLServer]
Driver          = /usr/local/lib/libtdsodbc.so
```

The second entry is for aesthetics only - so you can use "SQLServer"
in your connection strings. Odbc.ini file is not important if you use
fully qualified host names in your connection string but at your
option you may configure DSNs there.

FreeTDS

Also uninstall any system packages that you may have for this, then:

```
wget http://www.ibiblio.org/pub/Linux/ALPHA/freetds/stable/freetds-stable.tgz
tar xvf freetds-stable.tgz
cd freetds-0.91
./configure --with-unixodbc=/usr/local  --enable-msdblib --with-
tdsver=8.0
make
sudo make install
```

Remove or otherwise disable /etc/freetds.conf if any. Modify /usr/
local/etc/freetds.conf to read as follows:

```
[global]
        # TDS protocol version
        tds version = 8.0
        client charset = UTF-8
        # This is probably not needed...
        text size = 64512
# Typical SQLServer
[server_name]
        host = hostname.somedomain
        port = 2431
        tds version = 8.0
```

At this point you should be able to login successfully using:

tsql -S server_name -U username -P password

Here's what I do to fix the unspents of a tx.as_hex()
```
def fix_tx(hex_tx, spendables):
    print "fixing tx"
    tx = Tx.from_hex(hex_tx)
    unspents = []
    for txin in tx.txs_in:
        for s in spendables:
            if txin.previous_hash == s.tx_hash and txin.previous_index == s.tx_out_index:
                print "txin fixed: %s" % b2h(s.tx_hash)
                sp = Spendable(s.coin_value,
                            s.script,
                            txin.previous_hash,
                            txin.previous_index)
                unspents.append(sp)

    tx.set_unspents(unspents)
    return tx
```

The issue seems to be in here:

the Promise `p` executes function `do` in the context of the first drain_queue(self.normal_queue) when self.is_tick_used is set True

```
    def drain_queues(self):
        assert self.is_tick_used
        self.drain_queue(self.normal_queue)
        self.reset()
        self.have_drained_queues = True
        self.drain_queue(self.late_queue)
```

one global `async_instance = Async(SyncScheduler())` is used for all promises.

when the inner Promise calls its .get() -- it blocks indefinitely for the async_instance to call the secondary promise's `_settle_promise`

This blocks the first call to drain_queues infinitely locking up all promises :)

The build currently fails because of the inner workings of `cram`, not because of your changes. I was able to fix this by modifying the test (see 9188784). However, this revealed that the use of `python -m` breaks backward compatibility with Python 2.6 (see [Travis build 69](https://travis-ci.org/jgonggrijp/pip-review/builds/203799117)). Python 2.6 could only do `-m` with single modules, not with packages containing a `__main__` module.

At this point, I believe it is best to drop Python 2.6 support, as `python -m` is clearly the way forward. See the tip of the [feature/subprocess-call](https://github.com/jgonggrijp/pip-review/tree/feature/subprocess-call) branch, which builds upon your pull request. Since this is a breaking change, I want to include these changes with the 1.0 release. I will make an intermediate deprecation release to warn users that Python 2.6 support will be removed.

You pull request will remain open in the meanwhile, but rest assured that it is accepted and that it will be merged when the 1.0 release is near.

Standard:
results = face_recognition.compare_faces([person_encoding], potential_person_encoding,0.6)
Strict(Killed my false positives, but also removed some legit results): 
results = face_recognition.compare_faces([person_encoding], potential_person_encoding,0.5)

FWIW, I disagree with this change.

Most projects I work on keep tests outside of the library. I find the organization of tests at the top level more conventional.

The library code is intended to be consumed by the other projects integrating django-storages with their code. Contrast with the library tests which are intended to be consumed by the library developers. As they serve a different audiences, it seems like a good idea to keep them separate.

> For example, in dropbox backend, I use some fixtures for my tests and would like to reuse it for an other application's tests.

Tests and tests fixtures are not a public API and could change without warning as the library evolves. Unlike the library itself, test fixtures have no backwards compatibility concerns. Relying on this will eventually cause future frustration.

Thank you for indicate and explain. Thanks to you, I realized a mistake of my patch (commit for fix : ca285ec) and an oversight (commit for fix : dc5ac16).

I propose that unknown MIME types change from "text/plain" to "application/octet-stream". Because binary data may be broken by replacing "</head>" (line 207 of handlers.py on v2.3.2) if MIME type of arbitrary binary files is "text/html".

You wrote "the input data is always treated as binary", but I think your proposal may not work on Python 3. Because string and bytes are distinct on Python 3. Line 207 of handlers.py on v2.3.2 calls replace() with strings. And replace() raises "TypeError: expected bytes, bytearray or buffer compatible object" when bytes calls replace() on Python 3. But if strings of line 207-209 of handlers.py on v2.3.2 are appended prefix "b" to indicate bytes, the input data may be able to be always treated as binary on Python 2 and 3 (See https://docs.python.org/3/howto/pyporting.html#text-versus-binary-data ).

I cannot judge which proposals are good.
  * Unknown MIME types change from "text/plain" to "application/octet-stream".
  * The input data is always treated as binary.

I'm not sure I agree with your last point - lots of different ways of writing yaml are valid. But not necessarily meeting an organisation's standards. I could run `ansible-playbook --syntax-check` alone if all I cared about was validity. There are four options for you:

* Accept that my thoughts on indenting yaml lists are the One True Way, and use ansible-review's existing indentation check
* Say if it's valid, it's fine, and not even use the check at all (after all, all checks are examples, you can just ditch them)
* Write your own indentation check based on your organisation's needs (you could copy and paste existing check to your standards.py, fix the indentation checking for your own needs)
* Update the indentation checks so that they're configurable

Obviously I prefer the first ;) followed by the fourth.

Oh, one problem with your example: internally to deal with numerical instability, we normalize everything by removing the mean and dividing by the std. dev. This is turned on by default with the `normalize=True` param: https://github.com/CamDavidsonPilon/lifelines/blob/master/lifelines/fitters/coxph_fitter.py#L35

When you call `predict`, you have not normalized your data, so the values are all very extreme compared to what the model has seen. You can normalize like this:

```
from lifelines.utils import normalize
data_to_predict = normalize(df_test, mean=cf._norm_mean, cd._norm_std)
cf.predict(data_to_predict)
```

Cox: because the model is semi-parametric, there is not a definition of a full survival. What you can do to get a "basic" survival plot is to average all your training data over the columns, and run the `predict_survival_function` on new data point. AAF: Again, there is not really a full survival curve absent any specific data point, however the above technique would work for AAF too.

Seems like very specific case.
The easiest way to solve that is:

- add footer block to copy-pasted itemlist template
- extend your widget with a special method to return summary line values
- iter over it in your template

```python
class MyList(ItemList):
    @cached_property
    def values(self):
        ...
    def summary_values(self):
        yield from summary_raw_function(self.values):
```

ItemList is really depend on what return `values()`, I'm not sure there is an easy way to suggest a generic solution (to add it in controlcenter source code).

The migration needs to be reversible to be testable, because the test-case needs to be able to get to the state before it. It does this by migrating to that point, which requires it to migrate your migration backwards. If you don't care about the reverse operation, you can do:
```python
migrations.RunPython(fix_all_my_data, migrations.RunPython.noop)
```
The `before` and `after` options just need to be things that it can migrate to. So "before" just means "state to be at before we call `run_migration()`" and "after" just means "state to be at after we call `run_migration()`". There is (I think) no requirement that `before` is before `after`.

I would claim that it is useful to be able to test your reverse migrations. We might never want to run a reverse, but it may be the case that if a deployment goes wrong you will need to roll-back your database, and migrating my be preferable to loading a backup.

There is the possibility here of a problem where you specifically want a migration to be non-reversible, but you still want to test it running forwards. If that is relevant here, then ideas welcomed.

The value is just so hight to definitely be large enough for all phone numbers. And didn't see a performance impact in just setting it at this size. I guess something like 32 would have been enough as well.
Annoyingly this is not easy to change in hindsight. If we just change the value here, people using this field in re-usable apps will produce different database migrations based on which version of django-phonenumber-field they happen to have had installed when they created their migration. So it is likely that people will get warnings about missing migrations in a re-usable app (which they don't have control over), if they upgrade django-phonenumber-field in their project.

IMHO there is no significant performance reason to go through that hassle for this change. But I'm no database expert, so I may be wrong :-)

Hi @iradovanovic,
ES2L would not know whether the click event would caused any confirm dialog or not, hence there is no way to be able to determine whether the library should skip the ready-check or not - without the flag.

I modified your example into two different approaches as below.
```
*** Settings ***
# Wait Until Angular Ready will use implicit_wait as its timeout value
Library    ExtendedSelenium2Library    implicit_wait=5

*** Keywords ***
Delete Command Center
    [Arguments]    ${commandCenterName}
    Open Command Center
    ${locator} =    Get Locator By Text    ${commandCenterName}
    # Wait Until Angular Ready will be called internally by Click Element
    Click Element    ${locator}
    Click Button    ${commandCenterDeleteButton}    skip_ready=${true}
    Confirm Action
    Sleep    1    Wait to finish action
```

```
*** Settings ***
# Wait Until Angular Ready will use implicit_wait as its timeout value
Library    ExtendedSelenium2Library    implicit_wait=5    block_until_page_ready=${false}

*** Keywords ***
Delete Command Center
    [Arguments]    ${commandCenterName}
    Open Command Center
    ${locator} =    Get Locator By Text    ${commandCenterName}
    # Wait Until Angular Ready will NOT be called internally by Click Element
    # because block_until_page_ready flag is set to ${false}
    Click Element    ${locator}
    # Only needed for AngularJS application
    Wait Until Angular Ready
    Click Button    ${commandCenterDeleteButton}
    Confirm Action
    Sleep    1    Wait to finish action
```

Have you considered using [watchman-make](https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/watchman-make.html)?   We've recently added (available in the v4.8.0-rc1 tag) a `--run` flag to make it easier to use as an ad-hoc alternative to the traditional headless trigger mode that I think you're using.  (The best documentation for this can be found by running `watchman-make --help`, or you can read it from the source in https://github.com/facebook/watchman/blob/v4.8.0-rc1/python/bin/watchman-make#L119)

One of the differences between triggers and watchman-make is that the settling is computed inside watchman-make itself and doesn't impact the core of watchman.  I think this will give you some more control over settling and dispatching.

You may also want to model your notification tool on watchman-make; it is just a python script.  You should find it easier to build a better integrated notification processor by taking the guts of that script and adapting it more directly to your needs.

Your suggestion would assume that the `.so` files are all next to the `deepspeech` binary:

`install_name_tool -change bazel-out/local-opt/bin/native_client/libdeepspeech.so @executable_path/libdeepspeech.so ./deepspeech
install_name_tool -change bazel-out/local-opt/bin/native_client/libdeepspeech_utils.so @executable_path/libdeepspeech_utils.so ./deepspeech

The other line as well, but with the complication that the user has to provide `libsox` and we don’t know where it is. We can ask for it to be in a specific place. Like in `/opt/sox/lib/libsox.3.dylib`or next to the `deepspeech` binary: 

`install_name_tool -change /Users/build-user/TaskCluster/LightTasks/X/tasks/task_Y/homebrew/opt/sox/lib/libsox.3.dylib @executable_path/libsox.3.dylib ./deepspeech
`

I think this is a security-related library and as such releases require three properties:

1. releases can only be done by a specific group of people
2. releases are explicit and intentional
3. release tags are signed

I think that might be good enough. However we do it, we're going to want the release process and this library to be vetted by @jvehent and his crew. That's next on my list to coordinate.

Towards that, I think @johngian's process is fine as long as "draft new github release" is really "create a signed tag of the commit to release in the git repository and push it to github". Further, the release process should be documented somewhere.

Beyond that, I don't think it matters a whole lot how it happens as long as it has the above three properties.

Personally, I don't really care about release cadence. Security issues should get released asap. Everything else can be quarterly or whenever. We can release when people need it--that's fine, too. If releasing is too arduous, I'm happy to do it.

Boto3 is an entirely different code base than previous versions of boto and so while we (for the most part) have feature parity, using the library is quite different. You are trying to use it as if it were boto2, which will not work. Here is how you would perform [that command](http://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/reference/services/cloudfront.html#CloudFront.Client.get_distribution_config) in boto3:

```python
import boto3

cloudfront = boto3.client('cloudfront')
config = client.get_distribution_config(Id='string')
```

Please see the [boto3 documentation](http://boto3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/index.html) for more information, including a migration guide.

@TropicMike there is a customization that went in recently to help with this, but it's currently only exposed at the client (low-level):

```python
s3c = s3.meta.client
s3c.download_file('BucketName', 'RemoteFilename', 'LocalFilename')
s3c.upload_file('LocalFilename', 'BucketName', 'RemoteFilename')

# Example:
s3c.download_file('MyBucket', 'hello.txt', 'hello.txt')
```

You can see the full documentation and available features in the [boto3/s3/transfer.py](https://github.com/boto/boto3/blob/develop/boto3/s3/transfer.py) file. The client's `upload_file` and `download_file` are just a convenience layer so you don't have to make your own `S3Transfer` class from the client.

    guest_map.adapter = vim.vm.customization.IPSettings()
    guest_map.adapter.ip = vim.vm.customization.FixedIp()
    guest_map.adapter.ip.ipAddress = customeInfo.ip  # hard coded for now
    guest_map.adapter.subnetMask = customeInfo.subnetMask  # ditto
    guest_map.adapter.gateway = customeInfo.gateway  # ditto
    guest_map.adapter.dnsServerList = customeInfo.dnsServerList  # ditto

Hi Mark, PyJWT (correctly) uses `extras_require` expressly for this purpose, calling the optional cryptography-requiring functionality `crypto`, see:
https://github.com/jpadilla/pyjwt/blob/74399b/setup.py#L73

I just sent a PR -- please see #46 -- which fixes how nexmo should be spelling its dependencies. With that change, if you `pip install nexmo`, you can verify that `cryptography` still gets installed. But now the nexmo package is future proofed against `PyJWT` swapping out its underlying dependency for the optional `crypto` functionality, whereas currently if `PyJWT` were to do this, `pip install nexmo` would not end up getting whatever new dependency they switched to.

Thanks for taking a look at this! This was a bit more complicated than I originally realized, so thanks for explaining and hopefully you think the improvement I contributed in #46 is worth making.

Hi. I have a similar need, some of our tools still requires `requirements.txt`. I usually generate `requirements.txt` and `requirements-dev.txt` so they are available. I prefer generating a version-frozen requirements for application (i think it is your use case) and non-version-frozen requirements for library (so that readthedocs are happy for instance). But the ultimate source of truth is the `Pipfile` (for library) or the `Pipfile.lock` for applications. I have [this tool to generate these files](https://github.com/gsemet/pipenv-to-requirements) for me. The only drawback is to not forget to update them and track them in git.

I would love to see a setting in pipfile to generate these files for me. Maybe a script/plugin of Pipfile could be good. I didn't started to look at pep518, this will have a major impact on the python build system.

Here is my suggested fix:
- `SDRClassifierRegion` should have an _optional_ additional input, `valueIn`
- the `RecordSensor` region should have an _optional_ additional output, `predictedFieldOut`

The `RecordSensor` can use its reference to its encoder to determine a good `bucketIdx` for each value, and use that as `categoryOut`, as well as send the predicted field in `predictedValueOut`. That would perhaps require adding an `.enablePrediction(predictionArgs)` to the `RecordSensor`, much like is done [here](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/frameworks/opf/model.py#L155) for the OPF framework. `predictionArgs` can just be the name of the field, or have additional information (number of buckets to use if no encoder is used?).
Of course, a flag would be set by `enablePrediction`, and nothing is sent to `predictedValueOut` if this flag is not set.

As for the `SDRClassifierRegion`, it can do what it currently does if there is no `predictionInput`, i.e. use `int(category)` for both the bucket ID and actual value. If there is a `predictionInput`, it uses `int(category)` for the bucket ID (which, if connected to a sensor, is optimal as it is the same bucket as in the encoder), and `predictionInput` for the actual value.

**API**
> I think using fire with modules will be the most common usage

I actually suspect (just a guess of course!) that using it with files will be the most common usage. One reason for this is that everyone's default tab-completion completes files but not modules. Another reason is that I don't know of any command line tools that accept module specifications w/o a flag (e.g. python -m,  ipython -m, hug -m). Specifying modules at the command line just isn't a common practice (yet :P). This is why I think we should flip your 1 and 2 (check if it's a file path that exists before checking if it's a valid module specification).

To clarify my hunch some more: I think people will use this with things they work on themselves more than with builtins.

**Testing**
One of the beautiful things about making CLIs w/ fire (imho) is that you aren't left with a hard-to-test main method. Naturally I think we should make this CLI w/ fire. We need two things to make this possible: 1. We need to finish issue #53. 2. We need to be able to move "import *" into a function call, and I think [jtratner's comment](https://github.com/google/python-fire/issues/29#issuecomment-289332054) will let us do this. Once we do this, we can write normal unit tests for this CLI.

Related to testing: internally at Google we have additional tests that do test Fire CLIs by running them as separate processes the way you'd normally run a CLI (that is, the tests use `subprocess.Popen`). I didn't open source those particular tests because the way the CLIs are built/referenced is google-specific.

This seems to be a general solution for the primary `typing` objects:
```
>>> import typing
>>> import dill
>>> t = typing.Tuple[typing.Callable, typing.Any]
>>> assert t == t.__origin__[t.__args__]
>>> l = typing.List[int]
>>> assert l == l.__origin__[l.__args__]
```
Note that typed functions pickle, but lose their annotations:
```
>>> def doit(x: int, y: List[int]) -> List[int]:
...   return y + [x]
... 
>>> _doit = dill.copy(doit)
>>> doit.__annotations__
{'x': <class 'int'>, 'y': typing.List[int], 'return': typing.List[int]}
>>> _doit.__annotations__
{}
>>> 
```
Looks like `NewType` also pickles, except it gets the `qualname` wrong:
```
>>> UserId = typing.NewType('UserId', int)
>>> _UserId = dill.copy(UserId)
>>> _UserId
<function new_type at 0x10261fea0>
>>> UserId
<function NewType.<locals>.new_type at 0x10261fd90>
>>> _UserId.__qualname__
'new_type'
>>> UserId.__qualname__
'NewType.<locals>.new_type'
```
The pattern for a `Callable` is something like this:
```
>>> q = typing.Callable[[int,str,typing.Any], typing.List[typing.Any]]
>>> assert q == q.__origin__[list(q.__args__[:-1]),q.__args__[-1]]
>>> c = typing.Callable[[int], None]
>>> assert c == c.__origin__[list(c.__args__[:-1]),c.__args__[-1]]
```
`Generic` types don't seem to have anything new:
```
>>> T = typing.TypeVar('T')
>>> dill.copy(T)
~T
>>> T
~T
>>> g = typing.Generic[T]
>>> assert g == g.__origin__[g.__args__]
>>> m = typing.Mapping[int,T]
>>> assert m == m.__origin__[m.__args__]
>>> i = typing.Iterable[T]
>>> assert i == i.__origin__[i.__args__]
``` 
I didn't check all the objects in `typing`, but there doesn't seem (at a glance) to be any other quirky ones that might need a different pattern than above.

So the cases that  need some attention appear to be:
- most `typing` objects, like `typing.Tuple`
- `typing.Callable`
- `typing.NewType`
- functions with `__annotations__`
Oh, `importable` attempts to create source that can be saved to a file, so that you can import the desired objects from the newly created file.

Here are the steps I took to change the text color, in case you need to add other properties:

* Open the generated document in MS Word or LibreOffice and add a property by hand (in this case, change the text color).
* Save the document
* Unzip the .docx file (.docx files are normal zip archives).
* Go into the `word/` folder in the unzipped archive and open `document.xml`
* It's a bit of a mess, so search for the link text (in this case "Google").
* Look for the element that causes the property to change. For color it was `<w:color w:val="FF8822"/>` inside the `<w:rPr>` element. (Side note: rPr stands for "**r**un **Pr**operties")
* Add an argument to the add_hyperlink function
* Add a block of code that adds the property to `rPr`.
```python
    # Add color if it is given
    if not color is None:
      c = docx.oxml.shared.OxmlElement('w:color')
      c.set(docx.oxml.shared.qn('w:val'), color)
      rPr.append(c)
```
* Last step: **post your solution** so other people can benefit from it.

Here is the updated workaround with control of color and underlining:

````python
import docx

def add_hyperlink(paragraph, url, text, color, underline):
    """
    A function that places a hyperlink within a paragraph object.

    :param paragraph: The paragraph we are adding the hyperlink to.
    :param url: A string containing the required url
    :param text: The text displayed for the url
    :return: The hyperlink object
    """

    # This gets access to the document.xml.rels file and gets a new relation id value
    part = paragraph.part
    r_id = part.relate_to(url, docx.opc.constants.RELATIONSHIP_TYPE.HYPERLINK, is_external=True)

    # Create the w:hyperlink tag and add needed values
    hyperlink = docx.oxml.shared.OxmlElement('w:hyperlink')
    hyperlink.set(docx.oxml.shared.qn('r:id'), r_id, )

    # Create a w:r element
    new_run = docx.oxml.shared.OxmlElement('w:r')

    # Create a new w:rPr element
    rPr = docx.oxml.shared.OxmlElement('w:rPr')

    # Add color if it is given
    if not color is None:
      c = docx.oxml.shared.OxmlElement('w:color')
      c.set(docx.oxml.shared.qn('w:val'), color)
      rPr.append(c)

    # Remove underlining if it is requested
    if not underline:
      u = docx.oxml.shared.OxmlElement('w:u')
      u.set(docx.oxml.shared.qn('w:val'), 'none')
      rPr.append(u)

    # Join all the xml elements together add add the required text to the w:r element
    new_run.append(rPr)
    new_run.text = text
    hyperlink.append(new_run)

    paragraph._p.append(hyperlink)

    return hyperlink



document = docx.Document()
p = document.add_paragraph()

#add a hyperlink with the normal formatting (blue underline)
hyperlink = add_hyperlink(p, 'http://www.google.com', 'Google', None, True)

#add a hyperlink with a custom color and no underline
hyperlink = add_hyperlink(p, 'http://www.google.com', 'Google', 'FF8822', False)

document.save('demo.docx')
````

This function is the hyperlink equivalent of duct tape: It get the job done, but becomes harder to use when the complexity of the task increases.

`.iter_run_level_items()` is the right name for that method if we implement it. There is already an `.iter_block_level_items()` method in the works on Document, and this would be analogous. The main question is what it should do with all the possible items it finds. You can see there are many possible child elements, of which run and hyperlink are just two (http://python-docx.readthedocs.org/en/latest/dev/analysis/schema/ct_p.html). The items that may appear *within* a run is something else altogether; I'm not sure what we would name that :)

For the moment I'm thinking .iter_run_level_items() would generate items of three possible types: Run, Hyperlink, and RunLevelItem. The first two would be a run and a hyperlink respectively. The last one would be used for everything else and only give access to the element (probably as .element). As other run-level items were implemented, they could be added explicitly. I'm inclined to think that Run and Hyperlink should inherit from RunLevelItem, but we can get to that later.

Hi @TonyBeswick, I'm thinking you would be better off to compose a Document object in your own wrapper object rather than add additional fields to Document itself. The choice to use __slots__ on that an many other objects was a careful one and not one we're likely to reverse for this purpose. One of the things that happened quite a bit would be that folks would accidentally add new attributes by mistyping an attribute name or whatever, then wonder why their code didn't work the way they expected it to.

You can pass through any unrecognized attribute lookups to the Document object by using a __getattr__() method on your wrapper class.

Hi, sorry, I forgot to comment earlier as I've been quite busy. I think it'd be good for us to chat so that we're all on the same page.

We already know that we can store WARC data in ipfs, the question is how best to do it. I think that we should try to standardize on an approach for ingesting WARCs and exporting them back out out of IPFS. After our last chat, we were waiting on the IPLD implementation, but perhaps now is the time to revisit this.

For some background, there is this comment thread ipfs/archives#28 as the main thread for web archive related activities.  There is also an implementation I created last year ikreymer/pywb-ipfs which provides a full browse, write WARC to IPFS, replay from IPFS solution. This was a demo with the full record being stored. This implementation provides a different way of storing works, separating the headers and the payload, which I think makes sense. I think the goal now is to iterate on this to make sure we know exactly how we want to proceed with storing WARCs.
One idea that was mentioned was even creating a standard `ipfs warc add` tool.

Once we have this figured out, I think we can then proceed to build more tools to use WARCs and IPFS. I already have many ideas, but am holding off until we have a standard approach. :)

Related: a call to `predict()` on the sklearn model (after adding in the fix mentioned above: reshaping the coefficients) will raise an AttributeError:

```
# Convert to sklearn
converter = Converter(spark.sparkContext)
sk_model = converter.toSKLearn(model)
predictions = sk_model.predict(np.vstack(features))
print(predictions)
```
Raises:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
[...]
  File "<path>/.venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/sklearn/linear_model/base.py", line 341, in predict
    return self.classes_[indices]
AttributeError: 'LogisticRegression' object has no attribute 'classes_'
```
It looks like `convert.py` needs to be setting this attribute as well. The following fixes my example (for binary classification):
```
# Convert to sklearn
converter = Converter(spark.sparkContext)
sk_model = converter.toSKLearn(model)

sk_model.coef_ = sk_model.coef_.reshape(1, -1)  
sk_model.classes_ = np.array([0, 1]) # This is needed!

predictions = sk_model.predict(np.vstack(features))
print(predictions)
```
If these fixes look like the right way to do things, I'd be happy to submit a pull request.

If the other package named 'planet' also has an `__init__` that declares a version, the first `planet/__init__` encountered (as determined by PYTHONPATH order) runs and the other doesn't. If the version is not 'really' used, this is not a big deal but if the code is doing any version sniffing, it could cause needless confusion.

See 'Version numbers for namespace packages' here for conventions: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0396/#examples

Another resolution would be to rename the top-level package, e.g. `planet_api`

I didn't read the whole bug report, I thought issue was about buffering. Using a PTY to circumvent that is quite easy. Use `pty.openpty()`. Provide the first file descriptor to the subprocess and you can read unbuffered data from the second file descriptor. That's not exactly the same as doing flush after each line since you may read character per character. So, definitely not a solution if the problem is incorrect handling of truncated data.

* when inserting - postgresql does match the ids, and fails on unique constraint

Just weighing in on this.  I'm against this for a few reasons...

Although the current solution (also recommended by Heroku) of using an empty commit for this use case might feel somewhat clunky, the alternative (the suggested `deis apps:rebuild`) feels even clunkier to me for the simple fact that it's a _departure_ from the git-based deployment workflow.  IMO, the clunkiness that adheres to the promise of a "git-based deployment workflow" is better than the clunkiness that does not.

The whole notion of _rebuilding_ an app is predicated on the notion that builds are semi non-deterministic.  That is to say, building twice over some period of time might produce different results if, for instance, a new version of the buildpack in question or new versions of fuzzy-versioned dependencies are released in the interim.  Since the effective output of the first build vs the second are _different_ deployable artifacts, handling this in git seems the _right_ thing to do.  You may end up with an empty commit, but the message on that empty commit might contain an invaluable explanation for why the application was rebuilt.

On technical grounds, `deis apps:rebuild` would be very difficult to implement.  Currently, the builder does not store git repos anywhere that they will persist between reboots of that component.  What this effectively means is that if the builder component is rebooted, Deis no longer _has_ the latest source for your application.  To rebuild, you would have to be pushing it again, regardless.  To "fix" this would introduce a new dependency for the builder upon Ceph or some other distributed block or object storage.  Given the other two arguments above, I think this is a relatively high price to pay for something that we maybe shouldn't do.

Ah, we can't do that, I forgot. The problem is that build strings have no semantic content. So matching a later build's metadata to an earlier one, when there is an entire matrix of packages built for different Python or NumPy versions, cannot be done under current conda specs.

We would have to modify the conda spec to fix the notion of a build string as the combination of some sort of disambiguator _plus_ a build number.

To say a few more words on my earlier comment: as surprising as it sounds it is, from my 3 years of experience with conda, usually totally OK to upgrade packages in a way that on paper claim to break other package dependencies.  The main problems:

1. This is not what people expect, so it violates PLS.  Even Stan, at Continuum for years now, apparently was not aware of this.  In fact, in some ways it validates my comment: presumably Stan has not been bitten by this in his years of use of `conda`, until this point, so most of his dependency-breaking `conda updates` did not in fact stop his code from running. I try to educate people to this surprising fact whenever possible, but still, clearly it is something we need to address (https://groups.google.com/a/continuum.io/d/msg/anaconda/BK4nkmSieoY/9pKRZ4_RDAAJ)

2. When it does mean that the conda operation will break the code you want to run in that conda environment then you have no idea until code that did work stops working and that is just confusing.  For me, this clearly points to a need (as identified in some of the GH issues I reference above) to provide a warning that the operation will break other package dependencies (and ideally *how* they are being broken so the user can make an informed decision as to whether or not they are prepared to live with this).

3. Also as identified in some of the GH issues I reference above many package constraints are too rigid: either packages aren't strictly required, or the dependencies should really have followed http://semver.org/ (semantic versioning) and not had a hard requirement down to the patch level.  And now in the world of Python 3.4 and Python 3.5 in many cases `3.4 >= python < 4.*` (however you specify that in conda-dependency-speak) might be a little optimistic but probably totally acceptable, rather than the crazy situation we have now of creating py34 **and** py35 packages for everything even though they are identical.

4. We need to understand that we have two quite distinct use cases/user profiles, and we need to be prepared to serve them both:
    * the interactive, exploratory data scientist who can (if suitably informed) make decisions about package upgrades and having a conda environment where not everything might run perfectly at any given time, but so long as the last few `conda` operations are still "valid" (deps still satisfied), those are the ones they care about most, and please don't hold them back with artificial dependency constraints. This person needs `--relax-patch-deps` and `--nowarn-broken-deps`; and
    * the computer program or sys admin who needs total reliability, minimal deltas on package changes (if it ain't broke, don't fix it), and knowledge of exactly which packages are installed at any given time.  This person needs `--minimal` flag and `--strict-deps` (or whatever).

## Scope

Restructure error handling in `FastaFile.fetch`, `FastaFile._fetch`, and `VariantFile.fetch` to expose IO errors by raising Python`IOError` exceptions when `errno` is set.  The intent is to address hidden data loss (Issue #504), clarify when IO errors are occurring, and incremental to being able to add retry logic.  Testing is difficult, since simulating IO errors is not trivial.

Also added some missing detection of BCF allocation failures and raise `MemoryException`.

## Concerns

`FastaFile.fetch` still raises `ValueError` for non-IO errors.  However raising `IOError` is a non-backward compatible change and may cause problems in practice.  To me, is justifiable in order to unobfuscate when IO errors occur.

I actually had the same idea here: https://github.com/pre-commit/pre-commit/issues/149

My main (unvoiced I guess, since I just closed the issue without comment) concern with this would be the complexity of allowing two datatypes for exclude (and the subsequent confusion of "is this a list? is this a string?") -- so I don't really want to make that a list.  That said, I think I can make a small patch to make this work, which I think is just as readable (though it uses a yaml-specific feature):

```yaml
-   id: no_httparty
    exclude: >
        ^(
            path_to_some_file_which_has_a_good_reason_to_use_httparty.rb|
            some_other_file.rb|
            ...
        )$
```

As for a pep8 / rubocop -like feature, pre-commit only maintains a subprocess / file interface currently and I don't really want to get into the business of reading every file for performance reasons.  For the most part, if a tool wants line-specific exclusion, the tool itself should implement / document that (you may be able to extend your no_httparty hook to support something like that).

Let me cook up a branch for enabling multi-line regexes though :)

2) Make sure to reinstall jupyter: `pip3 install jupyter`

I was able to have some level of success with these translations.  But there are some things that weren't quite right..... Here's what I did:

1). The translated files are really .po, not .pot ( although the format is the same ), so I renamed them.
2). Put the translated notebook.po and nbui.po into notebook/i18n/zh-CN/LC_MESSAGES.
3). Run pybabel compile to create Chinese notebook.mo and nbui.mo in notebook/i18n/zh-CN/LC_MESSAGES per the instructions.
4). Run po2json to create Chinese nbjs.json in notebook/i18n/zh-CN/LC_MESSAGES
5). Update notebook/i18n/nbjs.json to add "zh-CN" to the list of supported languages.
6). Update notebook/static/base/js/i18nload.js per the instructions in the comment:

         // These are only here so that the optimizer knows which ones we MIGHT load.
	 // We will actually only load the ones we need. There should be one entry
	 // here for each language you want to support.
	 // For example, for German....
	 "json!base/../../i18n/zh-CN/LC_MESSAGES/nbjs.json"

7). Regenerate the javascript with "setup.py css js"
8). Set your language to zh-CN and your browser default to zh-cn.
Run jupyter notebook.....

In case you're wondering, the reason we're using zh-CN instead of just zh is that moment doesn't have a "zh" locale, only "zh-cn" and "zh-tw", so the date stuff won't come out right if you try to use just "zh".
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/4491110/30603118-2ea5ee76-9d2c-11e7-8036-76bc2b958b99.png)

Some things to be noted:

1). There are a number of places where the English has changed since my original drop, so in order to completely do this right, you would need to update the English using pybabel extract in the instructions, and then translate the updated files.

2). There are some places where the translations were incomplete or missing items.

But overall, these files seem to work OK and produce reasonable amounts of Chinese.

```

I wouldn't say that this is by design in the sense that it is a deliberate side-effect of the parsing, but I'm not sure it's a bug, either. The lexical tokenizer converts strings to unicode as the first stage in the parsing (see [here](https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/parser.py#L67-L71)), and so by the time the tzoffset is parsed, it's *already* a unicode string.

I think the same effect will generate unicode tokens when doing fuzzy parsing with tokens:
```python
>>> from dateutil.parser import parse
>>> date_str = 'Today is 2015-09-08, the last day to get tickets to the Eisbrecher show'
>>> isinstance(date_str, str)
True
>>> dt, tokens = parse(date_str, fuzzy_with_tokens=True)
>>> dt
datetime.datetime(2015, 9, 8, 0, 0)
>>> tokens
(u'Today is ', u', the last day of Septemberfest')
```

It's possible to "fix" this by setting some sort of flag in the tokenizer that remembers whether the original text was a binary or unicode object and then try and convert back, but is this actually worth doing? It's not clear to me that it's an actual problem to output unicode and the user can always convert it back later if for some reason they really want a byte string.

Its all related to how ZeroMQ socket behave. Which is used for zerorpc communication.

ZeroMQ does round robin load balancing. If you connect a client to 3 servers, every request will go on a different server. If one server is unreachable 1/3 of requests will queue up and eventually drop.

On the other hand, if the server connects to the client, ZeroMQ will round robin from the client to the 3 servers, but only while they are connected, thus, you are not blocking on unreacheable servers.

Finally, because ZeroMQ is message based and has no notion of session, you cannot logically group messages together. This means, that you must turn off zerorpc heartbeating when you are connecting multiple servers to one client. This is because the heartbeat message would endup being sent to the wrong server.

OK,

Well this unravelled to be a list of additional papercuts which all needed to be fixed before the main issue went away.

The main changes in this PR are now:

 1. I don't plan to make a test for the original condition in the PR, it's tricky and I'd like to give it some thought before putting it in
 2. I've now moved all knowledge of which objects have been fully loaded into memory into the Repository and am having the registry correctly poll for this. This fixes a chicken and egg issue that arises otherwise when the situation crops up that an object has to be loaded at startup and then made dirty to trigger a new index to be written in the future.
 3. The main index is removed after it's use on startup and only written again on shutdown. Minor performance penalty in starting a 2nd parallel session but this is minor and jobs don't get out of sync on dropped connections etc. #461
 4. The way of checking the number of subjobs is now a little more resilient as well as having the ability to check for all expected XML to be present on load. This fixes a potential bug later on of XML being missing only when working with a job which is trickier to debug/fix.
 5. I've added a setUpClass to the unittest to fix problems with previous tests making the environment dirty before we start running. This should probably be adopted by any tests not making use of this in some other way but that is for another PR.
 6. I've fixed the test for job resubmit in the tutorial to work on faster machines #503
 7. Documentation, I've added a lot of docstrings to the registry/repo layers which makes life a little easier.

As a result the determination of if a job has subjobs associated with it is now a bit more robust and there is an additional check that all of the XML are present before we load a job from disk. (This still allows for us to recover a corrupt/missing XML in favour of it's backup but we won't load a job with a missing subjob, this indicates some severe event occurred and requires some human interaction).

The loading of the XML from disk can probably be simplified even further in a future but some of the checks simply copied from old code are now fixed/removed as they got in the way of understanding this.

Whilst not just due to changes in this PR it's worth noting that:
Checking if an object is now fully loaded into memory is fully working and works in such a way that a Job object is always the same actual object all the way through the registry/repository and only has actions performed on the same object so we can perform checks using ```id(someJob)``` within this code reliably. (This works in front of the proxy as we also cache the proxy for the lifetime of the session too).

I agree, listing the option is a good plan. One more to add to the list: having a separate base class for featurizers that do not perform well. This makes:

1. No changes. All options that effect `featurize`, `feature_labels`, et cetera must be set in the initializer
2. Add a training operation (`fit`) to BaseFeaturizer. The training operation changes sets attributes of the featurizer that may not be known before training data is available.
3. Featurize produces both labels and features. Each entry (row) can produce different features, and the `feature_label` operation becomes redundant
4. No changes to BaseFeaturizer, and we develop a new class. Perhaps featurizers that generate a "bag of words" type feature are indeed different enough to warrant a different API.

Am I missing any?

Of this current list, I think Option 2 is the best (e.g., the `fit_transform` method proposed by @computron , if I am not misunderstanding his proposal):

- _Option 2 provides the easiest interface to ML libraries._ The main purpose of the BaseFeaturizer class is to transform materials data into a form that works with many machine learning libraries: a matrix of features. The current contract for `featurize` encourages users to build featurizers that will generate an easy-to-use matrix of features.

- _Option 2 will make it easier to update a model than Option 1._ As @computron noted, we now have featurizers that generate different features depending on the input data. If we choose Option 1, each time an training set is updated we will have to re-initialize the featurizer, which places a burden on the user to track what the other inputs to the constructor are. A separate operation that updates the state of the featurizer given only the updated dataset reduces the complexity of updating a class by providing exactly the needed behavior using minimal inputs.

- _Option 2 will preserve `featurize_dataframe` not affecting the featurizer state._ To be useful, `featurize_dataframe` must generate the same features each time it is called on different datasets. A featurizer that generates different inputs for a model given the training set and test set will complicate evaluating the performance of the model. To address this, we must have a separate operations that alter which features are being generated.

The downside of option 2 is that there are indeed some ML libraries that expect inputs besides rectangular matrices. Some libraries operate on bag-of-word-like representations, others sequences/graphs. The current `BaseFeaturizer` does not work well with these. Perhaps these will require a separate base class that does not force outputs to be table-like.

PS. Once we agree on a pattern, I can draft a 'Implementation Guide' for BaseFeaturizer that captures our plan. This will also fill issue #93.

This PR lets one declare that SELinux is disabled. If SELinux is declared as being disabled, then the `DisableSELinuxMixin` should never be invoked. And if SELinux is declared as being enabled, then the `DisableSELinuxMixin` can reasonably assume that SELinux is enabled. I can imagine some cases where this "reasonable assumption" won't hold true, but I think it's fine to rely on this assumption for now. Better to make an incremental improvement than revisit those assumptions in this PR.

My thoughts: 

- I'm not a fan of a ``set(type=TextType.Bold)``-style API. That's a Java-ism to me. There isn't any benefit in Python compared to a string: ``set(type="bold")``. Only now I have to import extra types (and google the naming of TextType).

- I do like staying close to raw ESC/POS. The `text_type` argument we currently have is our own invention, I don't see the need for that. Just let the user set "bold" and "underline" separately.

- I like simplicity. Boldness is a boolean, so I would suggest `set_bold(True)`. If the term used were "weight", I would still prefer ``set_weight("bold")``. For underline, which has single and double options, I would support: ``set_underline(True|False|single|double)``. 

- I like separating set() into distinct methods. I should be able to set the boldness without affecting the font.

- I also like to have a generic ``set()``method, because it can be used with kwargs. But I've been wondering if that ``set()`` should have any defaults, or if it only should write those commands that the user requested. 

   I get why ``set()`` tries to reset all other properties to defaults. But it's not clear why for example the line spacing would not be reset. I.e. what belongs inside set() and what doesn't? It might also be confusing. When I first started, I knew that an empty ``set()`` call is some kind of "reset", but I didn't know it's just python-escpos own idea of a reset.

  Basically, this is about whether we want our API to just map to a raw ESC/POS command, or whether we want the API to have aspects of a layout engine that executes ESC/POS commands for you.

   The second thing is certainly useful. I wonder if that, we could come up with something different entirely. Just some spontaneous ideas:
     - Like MagicEncode, we keeps track of state. If I call ``magic_set(bold=True)`` and then ``magic_set()``, the only command executed the second time is ``turning of bold``.
     - Even without tracking state, we could do: 
```python
         engine = LayoutEngine(printer_driver, defaults={bold: True, width=4, font=a}}); 

         # set everything to the default, except weight
         engine.set(weight=2)  

         # change the defaults for this block
         with engine.block(vertical_spacing=2) as block:  
             block.set(bold=True)
             block.text("this text is bold")
             block.set(underline=True)
             block.text("this text is underlined but not bold")

         # another API idea
         engine.text("this text is bold", bold=True)  
         engine.text("this text is underlined but not bold", underline=True)        
```
    
This way we have both; a low-level API where I can send ESC/POS commands without any magic (but with nice names), and an API that makes it easier to assemble the style changes I want to make for every line.

The high-level API may also provide things such as ``hr()`` to output a line that matches the column width. I'm also working on https://github.com/miracle2k/py-xml-escpos as an even higher-level construct, but some of the logic (like aligning text both left and right in the same line) are not just useful via XML; they could also be useful in code, e.g, say:

```
layout_engine.line(left="foo", right="foo")
layout_engine.columns("left", "center", "right")
```

Re: copying on a `Ref<const Matrix>`, my main concern is that there are likely legitimate C++ interfaces out there that accept arguments by `Ref<const Matrix>` as an accepts-anything-conformable argument, because indeed Eigen will make a copy for expressions or comformable-but-not-stride-compatible types.

So essentially we're trading off pybind11 making the interface more restrictive to python than it is to C++ against the legitimate desire to not want a copy.

I originally started playing around with argument passing to think about ways to have a sort of `argument_value_policy`. (This let to me getting sidetracked with #611 being the result), so that you could say something like:

`.def("whatever", &Class::whatever, py::arg("x") = argument_policy::allow_copy`

which would essentially resolve this by letting the pybind11 layer indicate whether it wants copyability or not (either default to allowing copy with a `arg_policy::no_copy`, or default to disallowing with `arg_policy::allow_copy`).

I'd prefer the names `type_error_print_repr`, i.e. dropping the `enable_` prefix since, as you point out, the "disable" equivalent is really the `_str` method; these aren't really about enabling/disabling something, but just about making an A-or-B choice.

lol [this person](https://thraxil.org/users/anders/posts/2008/03/13/Subprocess-Hanging-PIPE-is-your-enemy/) agrees:

> A closer inspection of the subprocess.Popen docs revealed a warning "Note: The data read is buffered in memory, so do not use this method if the data size is large or unlimited." I'd probably read that before and assumed that it was a warning about possibly consuming a lot of memory and being inefficient if you try to pass a lot of data around. So I ignored it. The STDOUT chatter of shell scripts that I was collecting for logging purposes did not strike me as "large" (65K is positively tiny these days) and it isn't an application where I'm particularly concerned about memory consumption or efficiency.
> 
> Apparently, that warning actually means "Note: if there's any chance that the data read will be more than a couple pages, this will deadlock your code." What was happening was that the memory buffer was a fixed size. When it filled up, it simply stopped letting the child process write to it. The child would then sit and patiently wait to be able to write the rest of its output.

About my idea/suggestion/fix (and also for https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/issues/1079#issuecomment-335873151 ):

> > separate (submitting that transaction & querying transaction receipt) into 2 API calls?  
> > 1st API call: Submit transaction, answer is transaction hash  
> > 2nd API call: with transaction hash given, return status  

> This was intentionally avoided.  
> Using two calls won't really help since the problem with   
> timeouts will just move from the 1st API call to the 2nd.  

Yesbutno.

I imagine the first call to submit the transaction(s), but not wait for anything, so it immediately returns, just giving a (list of) transaction hash(es).

However, the second call could then rather result in 2 different outcomes:

```
{
    "status": "complete",

    "channel_address": "0x2a65aca4d5fc5b5c859090a6c34d164135398226",

    "state": "open",

}

or 

```
{

}
```

which means for me as a user that I sleep a few seconds, and then try the 2nd call again.

Can you tell me the following?

1. How many cores are on the machine? What is the output of `import psutil; psutil.cpu_count()`?
2. What operating system are you using?
3. What Python distribution are you using (e.g., Anaconda, or the version of Python that came with the system, etc).
4. Does numpy seem to be using multiple cores? For example, if you do `x = np.zeros((4000, 4000)); np.dot(x, x)`, and then open `top` in another tab, what percent CPU utilization does Python have?

One common problem is that numpy tries to use a bunch of cores, and so when you run multiple tasks that all use numpy, they each try to use all of the cores and the result is inefficient (there is often no speedup).

To get around this, you might try doing

```
export MKL_NUM_THREADS=1
```

before running the script.

A good rule with widgets is to never mutate the traitlet values themselves. In this case, the problem is that you are mutating `OptDict`, but the `id` of `OptDict` is still the same. As far as widgets are concerned, it's the *same* dictionary. Thus, when you re-assign it to `option`, there has been no change.

To make this work, you want to copy `OptDict` and assign that copy to your dropdown's `options` traitlet:

```py
import ipywidgets as widgets
from IPython.display import display

options = {'XXX':0,'YYY':1}
dropdown = widgets.Dropdown(options=options, value=0)

options_copy = options.copy()
options_copy['ZZZ'] = 9
dropdown.options = options_copy
print(dropdown._options_labels)
```

---

At some point, we are going to have eventful dictionaries and lists in traitlets, and this won't be a problem any more. Till then, you're probably best off treating traitlet values (i.e. the *things* assigned to traitlets, like `OptDict`) as immutable.

I think it is the same issue and adding `plt.show` does fix it. But this usage case is extremely common and we have always made strong promises that interact will "just work" with matplotlib. I know *a lot* about Matplotlib, I wrote the original version of interact, and follow ipywidgets reasonably closely. Even I had absolutely no idea why it would behave this way. It actually took me a very long time to realize it was something specific to matplotlib and not interact in general. I just figured interact was broken so I had stopped using it in all my talks.

Even though this behavior changed in a major release, I don't think this is a change our users will tolerate (there is nothing that points them to the problem+solution) and I think we should fix it and release a bug fix for 6.x.

A comment on why Matplotlib+interact is so important. Most other modern plotting libraries that are based on JavaScript (Altair, BQPlot, Bokeh) are too slow to work really well with basic interact. Sure something like BQPlot can be hooked up to ipywidgets in more complex ways, but the attraction of interact is that all that isn't required for simple things. With matplotlib+interact, you get a long way with just interact. I have some really nice examples of matplotlib+interact based code that allows a user to explore a statistical model/etc with an extremely simple interact call.

There was some confusion in chat about whether this had already been done. Which makes sense, because look, here's some code in `connect` that closes the socket on cancellation:

https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/65729b121c55ff0aa1f5d43bd9cdcd6a81e54f0c/trio/_socket.py#L706-L713

However, this only handles a cancellation that occurs during that call to `wait_socket_writable`. There are other places in the method that a cancellation could happen. So right now, if a cancellation happens in `wait_socket_writable`, then the socket is closed, but if it happens somewhere else inside `connect`, the socket isn't closed. That's kind of confusing. The idea in this issue is to make it so that *any* cancellation inside `connect` should leave the socket closed. Basically this just means taking the `try`/`except` I pasted above, and expanding it so that it wraps around the whole method, not just the call to `wait_socket_writable`.

Conclusion: this can't be merged until someone merges from master.

Hi @burm87 , unfortunately overwriting the `command` for the LocalStack container doesn't work that way.

What you can do, though, is run a second side container to execute your script. In the example below, I'm using `busybox`, which is a very small image (alternatively, you can use `alpine`, `ubuntu`, or any other Linux image).

```
localstack:
    image: localstack/localstack
    container_name: aws.local
    environment:
      SERVICES: s3,kinesis
    ports:
      - "4572:4572"
      - "9010:8080"
      - "4568:4568"
init:
    image: busybox
    command: sh -c 'sleep 10; sh /project/init_localstack.sh'
    volumes:
      - "./init_localstack.sh:/project/init_localstack.sh"
```

Within your script, you can connect to the LocalStack container via its container host name (`aws.local`). Note that you should `sleep` for a few seconds until the services are up and running. 10 seconds is a good heuristic, but you may need to increase this value based on your environment.
